brief_document.title: Bidirectional visual canvas: code-to-diagram projection with live overlay
brief_document.purpose: Reframes RM-009 ('declarative DSL + visual builder') away from a separate DSL and toward a generated, always-in-sync graph model (RM-009 as designed here). Because a workflow is a typed function over a small known vocabulary (run / spawn / receive / all / race / sleep), the graph model can be generated from the source, carry per-node run identity for a consumer to overlay, and be regenerated from bounded structural deltas — a projection of the typed source, never a second authoring surface (ADR-014). The rendered canvas UI and the live overlay are the dashboard family (RM-007).
brief_document.task: Make the graph model a faithful projection of the typed source. In: extract a workflow's primitive structure from the package and emit it as a graph model automatically; identify each node by its correlation key so a consumer can overlay a run's events; and regenerate Gleam from bounded structural deltas such that it still type-checks. The rendered canvas UI and the live overlay are the dashboard family (RM-007). Out: unbounded diagram-to-code synthesis, the rendered UI, and any path that makes the model the authoritative artifact.

requirements.title: Structure extraction and automatic graph rendering
requirements.spec: WHEN rendering a workflow, THE SYSTEM SHALL extract its primitive structure (run / spawn / receive / all / race / sleep and control flow) from the package and render a graph automatically. The rendered node and edge set SHALL match the workflow's actual structure.
acceptance: Structure extraction yields the node and edge set for a workflow from the package.
Rendering examples/order-saga produces a graph whose node/edge set matches the workflow's known structure, proven by a diff test (C23).
The diagram is generated from the source, not hand-drawn.

requirements.title: Per-node run identity and bounded Gleam regeneration
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL identify each graph-model node by its correlation key (activity sequence position, signal name, timer id, child spawn ordinal) so a consumer can map a run's recorded events onto the graph. WHEN a bounded structural delta is applied to the model, THE SYSTEM SHALL regenerate Gleam that still type-checks. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT build the rendered canvas UI or overlay (deferred to RM-007), and SHALL NOT make the model the authoritative artifact.
acceptance: Each node in the extracted graph model carries the correlation key needed to map a run's events onto it (C22).
A bounded structural delta regenerates Gleam that compiles under gleam build (C24).
The model is a projection — the typed source remains the source of truth (CN6); the rendered canvas that overlays a live run is RM-007.

boundaries: SHALL NOT support unbounded diagram-to-code synthesis — only bounded structural round-trip that regenerates type-checking Gleam.
SHALL NOT make the canvas the authoritative artifact; the typed source is the source of truth (ADR-014).
SHALL NOT change the event stream or the package format.
SHALL NOT build the rendered canvas UI or the live overlay — RM-007 owns them; this brief delivers the graph model and the Gleam regeneration.
verification: Node/edge diff test against a known workflow structure.
Regenerated Gleam from a bounded edit type-checks.

adrs.title: Authoring: the typed Gleam module is the single source of truth; no separate DSL
adrs.decision: The typed Gleam workflow-plus-activities module is the single source of truth. The activity signature already carries the contract (its input and output types, codecs, and tier); the engine generates the rest — worker handler stubs, registration, the manifest activities list, codecs, schemas, wire-compat goldens, and test skeletons — and projects the rest — the diagram and the time-travel state. No standalone textual DSL becomes a parallel authoring surface, because it would forfeit the compile-time type safety that is Aion's core differentiator (the aion-flow guarantee that an invalid composition fails gleam build). A visual canvas is a generated projection of the typed source with a live execution overlay and bounded structural round-trip, not a second source. Rejected: a separate DSL or manifest as the source of truth — it re-creates the spec-vs-implementation drift the typed module already eliminates and trades a strong static guarantee for a weaker one.

checklist.text: A workflow's primitive structure (run / spawn / receive / all / race / sleep and control flow) is extracted from the package as a graph model automatically.
The graph model identifies each node by its correlation key (activity sequence, signal name, timer id, child ordinal) so a consumer can map a run's recorded events onto it.
Extracting an example's structure and diffing the node/edge set against the workflow's known structure matches, proving the graph model is derived from the source rather than hand-drawn.
A bounded structural delta regenerates Gleam that still type-checks; the graph model is never the authoritative artifact (CN6).

stories.text: As a non-coding stakeholder, I want a workflow's structure exposed as a graph model that stays in sync with the code and carries per-node run identity, so that a viewer (the dashboard, RM-007) can render and overlay it for me to follow and discuss without reading the source.

constraints.text: Generated code is regenerable and drift-gated: `aion generate --check` reproduces every generated file byte-identical or fails; no generated file is hand-maintained alongside the source (ADR-002 forbids parallel sources; mirrors the existing aion codegen --check).

No defaults in generated configuration (ADR-001): codegen never emits a retry policy, timeout, backoff, or any value the author did not choose; a required value stays a required hole, never a silent default.

The determinism boundary is unchanged (invariant 2): generation emits activity plumbing and handler stubs, never side-effecting bodies; generated workflow code still routes every side effect through workflow.run, and generated code introduces no wall-clock or entropy path.

Production parity: the dev loop and dev UI run the real engine, the real store, and the real event stream; there is no mock-only execution path whose semantics diverge from production, and activity mocking is opt-in per run, not a separate engine.

No second source of truth for introspection (ADR-007): time-travel, the determinism diff, and the live canvas overlay read the existing event history and deterministic replay; no parallel debug log or state store is introduced.

The visual canvas is a projection (ADR-014): it is generated from the typed source; any structural round-trip is bounded and regenerates Gleam that still type-checks; the canvas never becomes the authoritative artifact.

aion-toolchain shells out to the gleam binary and stays optional: aion and aion-server carry no compiler dependency; without --gleam-path the server deploys pre-built .aion files only (the DESIGN-OVERVIEW boundary).
Thin-workflow respected (ADR-012): generated activities thread sealed payloads plus thin facts; codegen does not reintroduce heavy decode or prompt rendering into the deterministic workflow process.

Library-code purity: clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings and cargo fmt --check pass clean; gleam format --check clean for SDK changes; no unwrap/expect/allow/expect-attr in library code; every touched file stays under 500 LOC excluding tests.

resolved_context.intention: Authoring a durable workflow on Aion should feel like writing one typed Gleam function and a file of typed activity signatures — and nothing else. Everything that is mirrored today (worker handlers, registration lists, manifest entries, codecs, JSON schemas, wire-compat goldens, test skeletons) is generated from that typed source; everything that is observed (a run's state, its determinism, its shape) is projected from the event history the engine already records. When this cluster is done, the gap between 'I have a typed workflow' and 'I have a durable, deployed, debuggable workflow' is a save and a glance, not a day of hand-mirroring four files that must agree byte-for-byte. The authoring language stays Gleam — the type system is the contract and Aion's sharpest edge — and the declarative and visual surfaces become projections of that typed source, never a second place to author from. This is the experience an author reaches for, the loop they iterate in, the lens they debug through, and the canvas a non-coding stakeholder reads, all anchored to one source of truth.
